metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the ADR AC, e.g. `test -f docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md && echo 'ADR present'`, so sign-off cannot occur without the file existing.
findings.finding: AC5 ('A recalibration-cadence ADR is recorded under `docs/adr/`') has no proving command; the single pytest suite (`test_impact_model_versioning.py`) will not verify the file's existence, leaving this criterion unverifiable at sign-off.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — fifth bullet; Scope/files line 'docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md (new)'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the ADR AC: `Proving command: test -f docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md && echo 'ADR present'`. Optionally add a trivial pytest fixture that `open`s the path and asserts it is non-empty, keeping all proving commands inside the single pytest invocation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the separation-contract inequality in section D and AC4: the plan writes `>= 0.30` but ADR-0035 (line 74) writes `> 0.30` (strict) — align them to the same operator so the A/B script gate matches the co-calibration contract.
findings.finding: The plan states the ADR-0035 separation contract as `median(HIGH) - median(NIT) >= 0.30` (non-strict, ≥), but the authoritative ADR-0035 text reads `median(impact_code[HIGH]) − median(impact_code[NIT]) > 0.30` (strict, >). The ab_impact_model.py script will be coded to one of these; if coded to >=, an edge case that lands exactly at 0.30 passes the script gate yet violates the ADR contract.
findings.location: Section D — 'median(HIGH) - median(NIT) >= 0.30'; AC line 4; docs/adr/0035-code-review-two-lane-tier-tagged-impact.md line 74
findings.suggested_fix: Change the plan (section D and AC4) to use strict `> 0.30` to match ADR-0035, or update ADR-0035 to `>= 0.30` if the non-strict bound is intentional — then express the exact operator in the ab_impact_model.py implementation spec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct section B's description of `change_fingerprint`: it already exists in `code_review/sidecar.py` (not added by the data-capture child) — clarify what the data-capture sibling actually contributes that is not yet present.
findings.finding: Section B describes `change_fingerprint` as a 'join key added by the data-capture child', implying it will be introduced by that sibling ticket. The function `change_fingerprint` already exists in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` (lines 24–44). This is a false scope-exclusion: the plan may under-specify what the data-capture child still needs to deliver, or a reader may incorrectly conclude the `change_fingerprint` in the payload is sufficient evidence the data-capture work is complete.
findings.location: Section B — 'alongside the change_fingerprint join key added by the data-capture child'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Section D should specify the test seam for `ab_impact_model.py` — e.g. expose an importable `run_gate(scorer_fn=impact_code) -> bool` function so the AC4 unit test can call `run_gate(lambda _: 0.5)` and assert it returns False / exits non-zero.
findings.finding: AC4 requires a unit test that proves 'a degenerate constant scorer FAILS (proving the gate catches a regression),' but Section D specifies `ab_impact_model.py` as a script that calls `impact_code` directly with no parametric scorer injection point. There is no specification of the test seam — how the unit test drives the constant-scorer scenario (subprocess with a monkeypatched module? an importable `main(scorer_fn)` signature? a helper that the test can call with a lambda?).
findings.location: Section D; AC line 4 ('Unit-tested: the current fixture PASSES, and a degenerate constant scorer FAILS')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section D: 'Expose `run_gate(scorer_fn=impact_code) -> bool` (returns True iff the gate passes). The `if __name__ == "__main__"` block calls `sys.exit(0 if run_gate() else 1)`. The AC4 unit test calls `run_gate(lambda _: 0.5)` and asserts False.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `ab_impact_model.py` exposes an importable gate-predicate function (e.g., `check_separation(scorer_fn, fixture_path) -> bool` or `run_gate(high_scores, nit_scores) -> bool`) so the AC4 unit test can call it with a degenerate constant scorer directly — proving the script's own gate catches a regression, not just re-testing `impact_code`.
findings.finding: The A/B script `ab_impact_model.py` is described as calling `impact_code` directly with no parametric scorer injection point, yet AC4 requires a unit test that proves 'a degenerate constant scorer FAILS (proving the gate catches a regression).' Without an exposed, importable gate-predicate function (e.g., `check_separation(scores_high, scores_nit) -> bool`) in the script, the unit test cannot invoke the A/B gate logic with a substitute scorer — it can only replicate the predicate inline, which tests the test's own logic, not the script's gate.
findings.location: Section D / AC4: 'Unit-tested: the current fixture PASSES, and a degenerate constant scorer FAILS (proving the gate catches a regression).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section D: 'The script exposes a `check_separation(high_scores: list[float], nit_scores: list[float]) -> bool` function (the exact predicate the `__main__` block calls before `sys.exit`) so the unit test can call `check_separation([0.5]*16, [0.5]*15)` to assert False (degenerate constant) and `check_separation(high_scores_from_fixture, nit_scores_from_fixture)` to assert True — testing the gate logic, not the scorer.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Section B or Section E's ADR should define the version-token naming convention (e.g. `<gate>-v<N>` with strictly monotone N) and enumerate the known valid values, so any future bump has a machine-checkable contract.
findings.finding: The `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` string token set ('plan-v2', 'code-v2', and any future values) is referenced as a corpus-segmentation key throughout Sections B, C, and E, but no naming convention, enumeration, or registry is ever specified. An executor adding a third model version has no contract for what token to use — and since the calibrator's filter is a plain string-equality check, a misspelled or inconsistently named token silently produces an empty filtered corpus with no error.
findings.location: Section B (IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION constant definition), Section C (--impact-model-version filter), Section E (ADR cadence)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the ADR (Section E) or Section B: 'Version tokens follow `<gate>-v<N>` where gate ∈ {plan, code} and N is a positive integer bumped atomically with any formula change. The module constant is the single source of truth; the calibrator accepts an arbitrary string but the ADR records the canonical set.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Section B should state explicitly whether the `schema` literal is bumped alongside `impact_model_version` changes, and how `latest_review_result()`'s schema guard should relate to the new version key (e.g. 'schema is bumped only for structural shape changes; impact_model_version tracks formula changes independently').
findings.finding: The plan adds `impact_model_version` as a NEW top-level sidecar key without specifying its relationship to the existing `schema` key (`plan_review_result_v1` / `code_review_result_v1`). `latest_review_result()` already has a schema-guard rule (`schema == 'plan_review_result_v1'`) that determines payload usability — a separate processing rule from the calibrator's version filter. The plan does not specify whether `schema` should be bumped when `impact_model_version` changes, nor how a reader that checks `schema` should behave when it encounters a future payload where the two keys disagree.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider exposing the A/B gate thresholds (min-separation, max-nit-median) as CLI arguments with defaults matching the ADR-0035 contract, so future contract revisions require only an invocation change, not a source edit.
findings.finding: The absolute separation thresholds embedded in ab_impact_model.py (median(HIGH)-median(NIT)>=0.30 and median(NIT)<0.30) are hardcoded with no CLI override, meaning a future ADR revision to the separation contract requires editing the script source rather than passing a flag — coupling the gate logic to the script's text in a way that is not self-documenting at invocation time.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `--min-separation` (default 0.30) and `--max-nit-median` (default 0.30) arguments to ab_impact_model.py's argparse block, document their defaults as 'ADR-0035 contract values', and use them in the gate condition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document an explicit rollback path for the sidecar `impact_model_version` key deploy (e.g., 'revert commit / toggle flag; consumers confirmed unknown-key-safe via audit of `grep -r REVIEW_RESULT` readers') and add an AC or note in section B confirming all sidecar consumers tolerate the new key.
findings.finding: The plan deploys a runtime sidecar change (new `impact_model_version` key in REVIEW_RESULT payloads) to a live system but specifies no explicit rollback procedure — no feature flag, no documented revert command, no verification that all downstream sidecar consumers actually ignore unknown keys.
findings.location: Section B (Impact-model versioning) and Scope / files: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py`, `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line rollback note to section B: 'Rollback: revert the sidecar constant + `build_payload` line; all downstream consumers confirmed key-agnostic via [reader audit / test]. Deploy is a single additive commit, revert is `git revert <sha>`.' Optionally add a unit test that instantiates each known sidecar reader against a payload that includes an unknown key and asserts no exception.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `ab_impact_model.py` provides value beyond the already-pinned `test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit` / `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` assertions in `tests/unit/test_impact_code.py`; if those tests already satisfy AC D's regression-detection requirement, replace the new standalone script with a pointer to the existing tests to avoid duplicate gate logic.
findings.finding: Section D's `ab_impact_model.py` duplicates logic already pinned as production regression tests. `tests/unit/test_impact_code.py` already contains `_median_gap()`, `test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit()` asserting the EXACT same absolute contract (`m_nit < 0.30` AND `(m_high - m_nit) > 0.30`), and `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap()` asserting the new model beats the old mean. The plan proposes rebuilding this identical computation as a new standalone script — a second implementation of something already tested in the always-run suite.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace Section D's `ab_impact_model.py` with a reference to the existing tests (`tests/unit/test_impact_code.py::test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit` and `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap`) as the separation-contract gate; the AC can point to `.venv/bin/pytest tests/unit/test_impact_code.py -k 'separates or gap_beats' -v` as its proving command. If a human-readable report (printing medians) is genuinely needed separately from CI, scope the script to only the report-printing portion, with the assertion delegated entirely to the existing tests.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm why `impact_model_version` must be a separate key rather than encoding the model version into the existing `schema` literal (e.g. bumping to `plan_review_result_v2` / `code_review_result_v2`); if the schema bump is infeasible (due to the schema-guard in `latest_review_result`), document the constraint explicitly so future readers understand why two versioning dimensions coexist.
findings.finding: Section B adds a new `impact_model_version` top-level sidecar key as a second, parallel versioning dimension alongside the existing `schema` key (`plan_review_result_v1` / `code_review_result_v1`). The only stated consumer of this new key is the single `--impact-model-version` filter in `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py`. Adding a new module-level constant and a new top-level payload field for a single call-site is premature abstraction under Rule-of-Three; the existing `schema` key already version-stamps the payload format.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the five existing `build_payload`-calling test files (`test_sidecar_enrichment.py`, `test_plan_review.py`, `test_code_review_ws4.py`, `test_code_review_artifact.py`, `workflow/test_plan_review_metrics.py`) as known consumers and confirm none use exhaustive key-set assertions that would fail on the new `impact_model_version` key.
findings.finding: Five test files outside the plan's named scope directly call build_payload (both plan-review and code-review variants) and assert on the returned dict's structure; none are enumerated as consumers requiring attention after the new impact_model_version top-level key is added.
findings.location: Section B / Scope section — unenumerated consumers of build_payload
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Scope section: 'Existing build_payload callers (test_sidecar_enrichment.py, test_plan_review.py, test_code_review_ws4.py, test_code_review_artifact.py, test_plan_review_metrics.py) use named-key reads only — verified no exhaustive key-set assertion; no update needed.' If any do use set equality, add the new key to that assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Section D whether `ab_impact_model.py` is an OFFLINE companion tool (distinct from the CI test gate already in `test_impact_code.py`) or a replacement, and explicitly acknowledge the existing `test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit` + `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` tests that already enforce the same ADR-0035 separation contract in CI.
findings.finding: Section D's new ab_impact_model.py script duplicates the separation gate already pinned as a production regression test in tests/unit/test_impact_code.py (test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit: median(HIGH)-median(NIT)>0.30 AND median(NIT)<0.30; test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap), scoring the same fixture. The plan does not classify this behavioral overlap, leaving it ambiguous whether ab_impact_model.py is redundant infrastructure or genuinely additive.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before implementing `ab_impact_model.py` as a new standalone CLI, confirm and document what it provides beyond the existing pinned `test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit` / `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` tests (e.g., scoring against live field sidecars rather than the static fixture) — or consolidate the enforcement into the existing test rather than duplicating it.
findings.finding: Section D's `ab_impact_model.py` repackages assertions that are already pinned production tests in `test_impact_code.py`. `test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit` asserts `m_nit < 0.30` AND `m_high - m_nit > 0.30` on the same fixture; `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` asserts `_median_gap(impact_code) > _median_gap(impact)`. The plan even reproduces the `_median_gap` helper logic. The plan's stated justification for a new script — that the 'old mean cannot score the fixture' so a relative gate is trivially passable — applies equally to the new script (which also uses an absolute gate, already present in pytest). Before adding a new CLI artifact to maintain, the plan should confirm the new script provides something the existing pinned test does not (e.g., running against live field sidecars rather than the static fixture), or consolidate with the existing tests rather than duplicating them.
findings.suggested_fix: If the script's purpose is purely to re-gate the static fixture, delete the script and point the AC at the existing tests. If it is meant to score live field sidecars (which would be a new capability), state that explicitly in the plan and scope the script accordingly, clearly differentiating it from the static-fixture regression test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify in Section B (or the Section E ADR) why `impact_model_version` is the right mechanism over bumping the existing `schema` key when the scoring formula changes — document the specific constraint (e.g., payload shape is unchanged so a schema bump would be misleading) that makes the new parallel tag preferable to the established pattern.
findings.finding: Section B introduces `impact_model_version` as a second, parallel top-level versioning dimension in `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecars, alongside the already-established `schema` key (`plan_review_result_v1` / `code_review_result_v1`). The `schema` key is the existing corpus-segmentation mechanism in this codebase (readers skip payloads whose `schema` doesn't match). The plan never justifies why a separate tag is preferred over bumping `schema` to e.g. `plan_review_result_v2` when the impact formula changes — the established pattern for 'old vs. new payload shape' in this codebase. Adding a second versioning axis with independent semantics (one for payload shape, one for scoring formula) is a novel pattern that should be explicitly justified against the prior art.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence rationale in Section B: e.g., 'A schema bump would signal a payload-shape change; the impact-formula change leaves the payload shape identical, so a separate `impact_model_version` tag is more semantically precise and avoids misleading existing shape-checking readers.' This resolves the prior-art gap without requiring a design change.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
